skill.name: using-argc-argcfile
skill.description: Guides creation and editing of Argcfile.sh scripts that use argc comment tags and argc --argc-eval bootstrap syntax.
skill.summary: Create and maintain Bash Argcfiles with correct argc comment tags, arguments, options, flags, and metadata.
skill.author: CGamesPlay

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell, URL, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns in SKILL.md. Review found documentation examples, external documentation links, and substring false positives. I found no prompt injection, hidden execution, credential access, or exfiltration behavior.

low_findings.title: Shell Execution Patterns Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The flagged backticks, command substitution, argc invocation, and sample script execution appear inside Markdown examples or explanatory text. The skill does not ship an executable helper or instruct the assistant to run commands automatically, but users should review generated shell before executing it.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are within fenced code blocks or inline Markdown that teach argc syntax. No separate script or runtime hook executes these examples on behalf of the skill.

low_findings.title: External URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to the public argc GitHub repository, specification, variables page, and examples. I found no code path that sends user data or secrets to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each URL is visible Markdown or echoed install guidance for argc documentation. There is no network client code, webhook, encoded payload, or credential reference.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Pattern Is Standard Shell Redirection
low_findings.description: The filesystem finding is a sample Bash availability check that redirects command lookup output to /dev/null. This is a common non-sensitive redirection and not file collection or modification.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line only suppresses output from command -v argc in an example script. It does not read user files, enumerate directories, or write persistent data.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged high-risk weak cryptography locations contain words such as description or @describe. I found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, crypto API, hashing workflow, or password handling logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Semantic review confirms the matches are documentation text and argc tags. No cryptographic algorithm or security-sensitive computation is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copyable eval Command Substitution Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents eval "$(argc --argc-eval "$0" "$@")", which is the standard argc bootstrap pattern. It is not malicious in context, but generated Bash using eval should be reviewed before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is real Bash eval usage, but it appears in a legitimate argc setup example rather than hidden execution logic.

content.value_statement: Argcfile syntax is compact and easy to format incorrectly. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code create valid argc command runners with clear tags and Bash patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains the required argc --argc-eval bootstrap pattern for Bash scripts.
Documents argc comment tags for descriptions, commands, aliases, arguments, options, flags, environment variables, and metadata.
Shows how argc creates argc_ variables for parsed arguments and options.
Describes required, optional, repeated, defaulted, and choice-based argument forms.
Clarifies when to use Bash defaults and required-value idioms with argc variables.
Points users to argc specification, variable, and example documentation.
limitations: Does not install argc or manage package manager setup.
Does not validate generated Argcfiles by running them automatically.
Does not replace shell security review for generated Bash scripts.
Focuses on argc syntax, not general CLI framework comparison.

use_cases.title: Create a Bash CLI scaffold
use_cases.description: Generate an Argcfile.sh layout with top-level metadata, arguments, options, flags, and the argc bootstrap block.

use_cases.title: Add subcommands to an existing runner
use_cases.description: Modify an existing Argcfile to add @cmd sections, aliases, and command-specific parameters without breaking argc syntax.

use_cases.title: Document complex command inputs
use_cases.description: Turn repeated values, choices, environment variables, and defaults into readable argc comment tags.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic Argcfile
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Argcfile.sh for a Bash tool named deploy-helper. It needs one required environment argument and one dry-run flag.

prompt_templates.title: Add options and defaults
prompt_templates.prompt: Update this Argcfile to add a required --project option, a repeated --tag option, and a default environment value.

prompt_templates.title: Design subcommands
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this Bash script into an argc command runner with build, test, and release subcommands.

prompt_templates.title: Review advanced argc syntax
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Argcfile for correct argc comment tags, choices, repeated values, defaults, environment bindings, and bootstrap syntax.

output_examples.input: Request to create a simple argc-based greeting command.
output_examples.output: A concise Argcfile structure with a description, name option, sample flag, positional argument handling, and the required argc bootstrap guidance.

output_examples.input: Request to add choices and repeated options to an existing CLI.
output_examples.output: Updated argc tag guidance that identifies required values, repeated values, allowed choices, and safe Bash variable access patterns.

output_examples.input: Request to troubleshoot a confusing Argcfile.
output_examples.output: A review summary that explains which comment tags are malformed, which variables argc will create, and which defaults need adjustment.

best_practices: Keep argc comment tags close to the function or command they configure.
Use Bash required-value and default-value expansion for argc variables.
Review generated shell scripts before running them in a real project.
anti_patterns: Do not treat default or choice functions as arbitrary external commands.
Do not omit the argc availability check when sharing scripts with other users.
Do not mix unrelated shell parsing frameworks inside the same Argcfile.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps create and edit Argcfile.sh files that use argc comment tags for Bash command runners.

faq.question: Does it run argc automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides syntax guidance and examples. Users decide when to run generated scripts.

faq.question: Can it add subcommands?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains @cmd and related tags for structuring subcommands and aliases.

faq.question: Does it support environment variables?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents @env tags and environment binding patterns used by argc.

faq.question: Is the eval pattern safe?
faq.answer: It is the standard argc bootstrap pattern, but generated Bash should be reviewed before execution.

faq.question: Where does it point for more detail?
faq.answer: It links to argc GitHub documentation for the specification, predefined variables, and examples.
